title: The dependence of urban tick and Lyme disease hazards on the hinterlands

data_scope.spatial_coverage: 16 UK small cities (pop. 50,000-100,000), northern Scotland to south-central England and Wales
data_scope.landcover_source: UKCEH Land Cover Map 2021 (Land Parcels, GB)
data_scope.rings: city 0-5 km, hinterland 5-10 km, interface 4-6 km

core_conclusion: Across 16 UK cities, urban greenspaces have two- to threefold lower tick and Lyme disease hazards than nearby hinterland woodlands. Variation in a city's hazard is driven by the tick abundance and landcover (woodland, built-up) of its rural hinterland 5-10 km out, not by the city's own internal landcover or urban-hinterland connectivity, showing that cities and their surroundings form a coupled meta-ecosystem.
why_it_matters: Reframes urban disease risk as a regional rather than within-city problem; shows urban parks are 2-3x safer than rural woodlands yet matter at population level due to footfall; first meta-ecosystem application to urban terrestrial disease ecology; argues for cross-boundary urban-rural governance.
why_nature: Generalizable principle from 16 cities rather than a single case; couples space, ecology and human health; imports the meta-ecosystem concept into urban eco-epidemiology; counter-intuitive and policy-translatable 'look outside the city' finding.
added_value_to_my_research: Learned distance-weighted hinterland kernel -> host-specific decay for 2SFCA/R2SFCA catchments
Explainable ML ensemble + SHAP -> auditable hazard surface for unsurveyed cities
Footfall-weighted hazard -> realized human exposure and exposure-equity mapping via mobility fusion
Physics-informed host-tick graph with source-sink prior -> disentangle dispersal mechanisms
Fine-tuned geospatial foundation model -> national remote-sensing hazard surface (FAIR/reproducible)
